Looking at this, the breakthrough idea is well-motivated by amyloid biology: surface-binding inhibitors (Brichos, etc.) selectively suppress secondary nucleation/elongation while primary nucleation is nearly unaffected. The current best applies suppression to BOTH channels globally.

Let me implement the selective-channel suppression: apply the Langmuir-occupancy suppression ONLY to the autocatalytic (elongation/secondary-nucleation) channel, leaving the nucleation floor uninhibited.

Let me verify my understanding of the physics with a quick check of the reference file already present.
The local reference is essentially empty (147 bytes). Let me do one targeted research call to confirm the selective-inhibition principle before finalizing, as recommended.

The change: apply suppression **only** to the autocatalytic (secondary-nucleation/elongation) channel, leaving the nucleation floor uninhibited. This keeps cd=0 clean (suppression=1) and lets high-dose curves delay only the timing-controlling autocatalytic burst.
